howItWorks.intro: This audio sample rate calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: File Size = Sample Rate x Bit Depth x Channels x Duration / 8
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What sample rate should I record at?
faq.answer: 44.1 kHz is sufficient for most music (captures up to 22 kHz, beyond human hearing). Record at 48 kHz for video projects, or 96 kHz if you plan heavy pitch-shifting or time-stretching in post-production.

faq.question: Does higher bit depth mean better sound?
faq.answer: Higher bit depth means more dynamic range (quieter noise floor), not necessarily better sound. 16-bit gives 96 dB range (fine for playback). 24-bit gives 144 dB range — essential for recording to capture quiet details without noise.

faq.question: Is the Audio Sample Rate Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard music and audio formulas trusted by musicians and audio engineers. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, the Audio Sample Rate Calculator is fully responsive and works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download needed.

sections.title: What Is the Audio Sample Rate Calculator?
sections.body: The Audio Sample Rate Calculator is a free online tool designed for musicians and audio engineers who need quick, accurate calculations in the music and audio space. By entering your sample rate, bit depth, channels, you get instant results including uncompressed file size, bit rate, max frequency captured. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting uncompressed file size right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In music and audio, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by musicians and audio engineers worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to fine-tune your sound with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your sample rate and need to find the right uncompressed file size. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Sample Rate
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your sample rate — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: bit depth, channels, duration. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your uncompressed file size, bit rate, max frequency captured. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your sample rate before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both uncompressed file size and bit rate — they work together to give you the full picture.
